---
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    css: ../course-outline.css
    slide-number: true
    center: false
    margin: 0.05
    footer: "<a href='javascript:history.back()'>← Back</a>"
---

```{=html}
<section class="slide level2">
<h2>V. Jesus: Grace and Truth vs. Moses and the Law</h2>

<div class="lv-a"><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=TlX-m4KfD90" target="_blank">Opening Song</a></div>
<div class="lv-a"><a href="https://bibleproject.com/videos/law/" target="_blank">Overview Video</a></div>

<details>
<summary><strong>Opening Passage — John 1:17</strong></summary>
<div class="lv-lc"><a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A17&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:17</a> — "For the law was given through Moses. Grace and truth were realized through Jesus Christ."</div>
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>A. Different Christian Views on the Law</strong></summary>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Thomas Aquinas / Roman Catholic</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The moral law remains because it reflects God's eternal righteousness and natural law</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Christ fulfilled the moral law not by ending it, but by bringing it to its fullness—perfectly obeying it, revealing its deepest meaning, and enabling believers to fulfill it through grace</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Ceremonial laws were fulfilled and ceased because they pointed to Christ; judicial laws ceased as binding laws when Israel's theocratic state ended</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">John Calvin / Reformed Theology</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Christ fulfilled the moral law by perfectly obeying it and by confirming its continuing authority</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The moral law (especially summarized in the Ten Commandments) remains the rule of life for believers</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Ceremonial laws were fulfilled and abolished in Christ; civil laws expired with Israel's national covenant, though their principles of justice remain instructive</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Lutheran Theology</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Similar to Calvin in affirming the continuing validity of God's moral will, though generally placing greater emphasis on the distinction between Law and Gospel</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The moral law continues to expose sin and guide Christian living</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Dispensational Theology</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The Mosaic Law as a covenant has ended</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Christians are not under the Law of Moses but under the Law of Christ</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Moral commands continue where they are reaffirmed in the New Testament, not because the Mosaic moral law remains binding</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">New Covenant Theology</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Rejects the traditional threefold division of the law</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The entire Mosaic covenant has been fulfilled and brought to an end in Christ</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Christians are governed by the Law of Christ</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Many commands found in the Ten Commandments continue because they are repeated by Christ and the apostles, not because the Decalogue itself remains binding</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Eastern Orthodox</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The Orthodox Church generally does not emphasize the Western threefold division of moral, ceremonial, and civil law as strongly as Catholic and Reformed theology</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Christ fulfilled the Law by accomplishing its purpose and bringing humanity into union with God (theosis)</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The commandments are not viewed primarily as legal obligations but as guides to life in Christ</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The moral teachings of the Old Testament continue because they express God's character and are reaffirmed and transformed in Christ</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— The ceremonial aspects of the Mosaic covenant are fulfilled in Christ and find their completion in the worship and sacramental life of the Church</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Messianic Jewish Perspectives</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Jewish believers may continue Torah practices voluntarily</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Salvation remains through Christ alone</div>
  </details>
</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>B. The Pharisees and the Law</strong></summary>

  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Old Testament foundation — Torah as covenant life</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Deuteronomy+6%3A4-9&version=WEB" target="_blank">Deuteronomy 6:4–9</a> — The Shema: love God with heart, soul, and might; teach the Torah diligently. The Law as the core of covenant love and daily instruction.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Deuteronomy+4%3A5-8&version=WEB" target="_blank">Deuteronomy 4:5–8</a> — The Law as wisdom, justice, and distinctiveness among the nations.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Joshua+1%3A8&version=WEB" target="_blank">Joshua 1:8</a> — "This Book of the Law shall not depart from your mouth… meditate on it day and night." Meditation and obedience were central.</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Gezerot — fences around the Torah</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Rabbinic decrees enacted to prevent accidental violations of biblical commands</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Pharisees saw them as harmonious extensions that protected and fulfilled the Torah</div>
    <details><summary class="lv-lc">— The 39 Melakhot</summary>
      <div class="lv-detail"><strong>The Order of Bread (Agricultural / Food Preparation)</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">1. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Ploughing" target="_blank">Ploughing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">2. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Planting" target="_blank">Planting</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">3. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Reaping" target="_blank">Reaping</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">4. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Gathering" target="_blank">Gathering</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">5. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Threshing_.2F_extraction" target="_blank">Threshing / Extraction</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">6. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Winnowing" target="_blank">Winnowing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">7. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Sorting_.2F_purification" target="_blank">Sorting / Purification</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">8. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Grinding" target="_blank">Grinding</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">9. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Sifting" target="_blank">Sifting</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">10. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Kneading" target="_blank">Kneading</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">11. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Baking_.2F_Cooking" target="_blank">Baking / Cooking</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail"><strong>The Order of Fabric / Garments</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">12. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Shearing" target="_blank">Shearing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">13. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Scouring_.2F_Laundering" target="_blank">Scouring / Laundering</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">14. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Combing" target="_blank">Combing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">15. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Dyeing" target="_blank">Dyeing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">16. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Spinning" target="_blank">Spinning</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">17. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Warping" target="_blank">Warping</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">18. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Making_two_loops_.2F_threading_heddles" target="_blank">Making two loops / Threading heddles</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">19. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Weaving" target="_blank">Weaving</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">20. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Separating_two_threads" target="_blank">Separating two threads</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">21. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Tying" target="_blank">Tying</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">22. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Untying" target="_blank">Untying</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">23. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Sewing" target="_blank">Sewing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">24. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Tearing" target="_blank">Tearing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail"><strong>The Order of Hides / Leather</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">25. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Trapping" target="_blank">Trapping</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">26. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Killing" target="_blank">Killing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">27. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Flaying_.2F_Skinning" target="_blank">Flaying / Skinning</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">28. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Curing_.2F_Preservation" target="_blank">Curing / Preservation</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">29. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Smoothing" target="_blank">Smoothing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">30. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Scoring" target="_blank">Scoring</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">31. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Measured_cutting" target="_blank">Measured Cutting</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail"><strong>The Order of Construction</strong></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">32. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Writing" target="_blank">Writing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">33. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Erasing" target="_blank">Erasing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">34. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Construction" target="_blank">Construction</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">35. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Demolition" target="_blank">Demolition</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">36. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Final_Completion_.2F_Fine-tuning_.2F_Perfecting" target="_blank">Final Completion / Fine-tuning / Perfecting</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">37. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Ignition" target="_blank">Ignition (Kindling)</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">38. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Extinguishing" target="_blank">Extinguishing</a></div>
      <div class="lv-detail">39. <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/39_Melakhot#Transferring_between_domains" target="_blank">Transferring between domains (Carrying)</a></div>
    </details>
    <div class="lv-lc">— Categories of prohibited Sabbath work derived from activities used to build the Tabernacle; gezerot expanded these into detailed practical rules (e.g., healing, carrying objects) that governed daily observance — not written down until the 2nd century (Mishnah) but existing mostly as oral tradition in Jesus' day</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Jesus disputes the Pharisees</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+3%3A1-6&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 3:1–6</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+13%3A10-17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 13:10–17</a> — Pharisees classified healing as prohibited "work" (one of the 39 melachot). Jesus repeatedly healed on the Sabbath.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+2%3A27-28&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 2:27–28</a> — "The Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath. The Son of Man is lord even of the Sabbath." His healings exposed how gezerot prioritized rigid rules over mercy.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+5%3A1-18&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 5:1–18</a> — Jesus healed a man paralyzed 38 years and told him to carry his mat. Leaders objected not to the healing but to the "work" of carrying the mat — a violation of gezerot about carrying objects on the Sabbath.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+5%3A17&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 5:17</a> — Jesus defended His actions: "My Father is working until now, and I am working." The tradition protected the Sabbath's external form but missed its deeper purpose and the opportunity to celebrate God's mercy.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+7%3A1-13&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 7:1–13</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+15%3A1-9&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 15:1–9</a> — Jesus debates "the tradition of the elders": some gezerot nullified the Torah's intent (e.g., the Corban vow), while Pharisees saw them as upholding it.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">f. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+23%3A23-24&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 23:23–24</a> — Jesus rebukes placing ritual minutiae above compassion, justice, and the weightier matters of the Law: "You tithe mint and dill and cumin, and have neglected the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness."</div>
  </details>

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>C. The Law: Its Purpose and Limits</strong></summary>

  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">The Law revealed sin but could not save</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+3%3A20&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 3:20</a> — "For through the law comes the knowledge of sin."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+7%3A7&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 7:7</a>, <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+7%3A12&version=WEB" target="_blank">7:12</a> — "I would not have known sin except through the law." Yet "the law is holy, and the commandment is holy and righteous and good."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+3%3A10-11&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 3:10–11</a> — Those relying on works of the law are under a curse; no one is justified by the law.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+3%3A21&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 3:21</a> — "If a law had been given which could make alive, most certainly righteousness would have been of the law."</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Law does not change God's covenant to Abraham</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+3%3A15-17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 3:15–17</a> — Paul argues from ordinary human practice: once a covenant is ratified, it cannot be annulled or altered. God made a covenant of promise with Abraham. The Mosaic Law, given 430 years later, cannot annul that earlier covenant, add conditions to it, or replace it. The inheritance still comes by promise, not by law.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+3%3A23-25&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 3:23–25</a> — "Before faith came, we were kept in custody under the law… The law has become our tutor [<a href="https://www.biblestudytools.com/lexicons/greek/nas/paidagogos.html" target="_blank"><em>paidagōgos</em></a>] to bring us to Christ… But now that faith has come, we are no longer under a tutor."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+4%3A4-5&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 4:4–5</a> — God sent His Son to redeem those under the law.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+10%3A4&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 10:4</a> — "Christ is the end [goal/fulfillment] of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+5%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 5:1</a> — "For freedom Christ has set us free."</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">The heart of the Law — what God really requires</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Micah+6%3A6-8&version=WEB" target="_blank">Micah 6:6–8</a> — "What does the LORD require of you but to do justice, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with your God?" Perhaps the clearest OT summary of God's desires: justice, hesed (covenant lovingkindness), humble fellowship with God.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+22%3A37-40&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 22:37–40</a> — Jesus summarizes the entire Law in two commands: love God with all your heart, soul, and mind (quoting Deut 6:5); love your neighbor as yourself (quoting Lev 19:18). "On these two commandments depend all the Law and the Prophets."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Deuteronomy+6%3A4-5&version=WEB" target="_blank">Deuteronomy 6:4–5</a> — The Shema: the foundation of Israel's covenant is not legal obligation but love — "You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your might."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Leviticus+19%3A18&version=WEB" target="_blank">Leviticus 19:18</a> — "You shall love your neighbor as yourself." This command appears in the Law itself and becomes central to Jesus' teaching.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+23%3A23&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 23:23</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+11%3A42&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 11:42</a> — Jesus rebukes the Pharisees for neglecting "the weightier matters of the law: justice and mercy and faithfulness." He does not say the Law is wrong — he says they missed its heart.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">f. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Hosea+6%3A6&version=WEB" target="_blank">Hosea 6:6</a> — "I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice, the knowledge of God rather than burnt offerings." Jesus quotes this twice (Matt 9:13; 12:7), prioritizing covenant love and knowing God over ritual observance.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">g. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Jeremiah+7%3A22-23&version=WEB" target="_blank">Jeremiah 7:22–23</a> — "I did not speak to your fathers concerning burnt offerings and sacrifices. But this command I gave them: 'Obey my voice, and I will be your God, and you shall be my people.'" Relationship was primary.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">h. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Deuteronomy+10%3A12-13&version=WEB" target="_blank">Deuteronomy 10:12–13</a> — "What does the LORD your God require of you, but to fear the LORD… to walk in all his ways, to love him, to serve the LORD your God with all your heart and with all your soul." Emphasis on love, walking with God, and serving from the heart — not merely external compliance.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">i. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Isaiah+58%3A6-7&version=WEB" target="_blank">Isaiah 58:6–7</a> — God rejects ritual divorced from compassion: "Is not this the fast that I choose: to loose the bonds of wickedness… to share your bread with the hungry?"</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">j. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Zechariah+7%3A9-10&version=WEB" target="_blank">Zechariah 7:9–10</a> — "Render true judgments, show kindness and mercy… do not oppress the widow, the fatherless, the sojourner, or the poor." Echoes Micah and Hosea.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">k. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1+Samuel+15%3A22&version=WEB" target="_blank">1 Samuel 15:22</a> — "To obey is better than sacrifice." Relationship and obedience placed above ritual.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">l. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Psalm+51%3A16-17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Psalm 51:16–17</a> — "The sacrifices of God are a broken spirit; a broken and contrite heart." God wants transformed hearts.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">m. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+13%3A8-10&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 13:8–10</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+5%3A14&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 5:14</a> — Paul: "He who loves another has fulfilled the law." "The whole law is fulfilled in one word: 'You shall love your neighbor as yourself.'"</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">n. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=James+1%3A27&version=WEB" target="_blank">James 1:27</a> — "Religion that is pure and undefiled before God… is to visit orphans and widows in their affliction, and to keep oneself unstained from the world."</div>
  </details>

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>D. Salvation Is by Grace Through Faith</strong></summary>

  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Children of God by new birth</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+1%3A12-13&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 1:12–13</a> — "To all who received him… he gave the right to become God's children… who were born… of God."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+3%3A3-8&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 3:3–8</a> — Born again of the Spirit.</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Salvation is a gift, not earned</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Ephesians+2%3A8-9&version=WEB" target="_blank">Ephesians 2:8–9</a> — "For by grace you have been saved through faith… not of works."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Titus+3%3A5&version=WEB" target="_blank">Titus 3:5</a> — "He saved us — not by works of righteousness… but according to his mercy — through the washing of regeneration and renewing by the Holy Spirit."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+2%3A16&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 2:16</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+3%3A28&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 3:28</a> — Justified by faith apart from works of the law.</div>
  </details>

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>E. Jesus' Encounters: The Law Is Not Enough — Follow Me</strong></summary>


  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">The Rich Young Ruler</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+19%3A17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 19:17</a> — "If you would enter life, keep the commandments." The young man claimed to have kept them all.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+19%3A21-26&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 19:21–26</a> — "If you would be perfect, go, sell what you possess and give to the poor… and come, follow me." He went away sorrowful. "It is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God." "With man this is impossible, but with God all things are possible."</div>
    <div class="lv-detail">— External commandment-keeping cannot save; wealth had become his idol; true life requires surrendering everything to follow Jesus.</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Other encounters</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+12%3A28-34&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 12:28–34</a> — The Scribe affirmed love for God and neighbor; Jesus said he was "not far from the kingdom of God" — sincere agreement with the Law still falls short of entering through Christ.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+16%3A24&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 16:24</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Luke+9%3A23&version=WEB" target="_blank">Luke 9:23</a> — "Deny yourself, take up your cross, and follow me." Discipleship above all religious duty.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=John+14%3A6&version=WEB" target="_blank">John 14:6</a> — "I am the way, the truth, and the life." The Law is good but cannot give life; Jesus is the way.</div>
  </details>

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>F. Christ Fulfilled and Replaced the Law</strong></summary>

  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Jesus fulfilled the Law</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Matthew+5%3A17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Matthew 5:17</a> — "I didn't come to destroy, but to fulfill."</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">The shadow and the reality</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Hebrews+10%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Hebrews 10:1</a> — "The law, having a shadow of the good things to come, not the very image of the things…"</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Hebrews+10%3A1-14&version=WEB" target="_blank">Hebrews 10:1–14</a> — Christ as the final sacrifice accomplishing what the Old Covenant pointed toward.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Colossians+2%3A16-17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Colossians 2:16–17</a> — Festivals, new moons, Sabbaths are "a shadow of things to come; the body is Christ."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Hebrews+9%3A11-15&version=WEB" target="_blank">Hebrews 9:11–15</a> — Christ as mediator of a better covenant.</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Released from the Law — the marriage illustration (Romans 7)</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+7%3A2-6&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 7:2–6</a> — "A married woman is bound by law to her husband while he lives, but if the husband dies, she is discharged… You also were made dead to the law through the body of Christ… Now we have been discharged from the law… we serve in newness of the Spirit."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+8%3A1&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 8:1</a> — "There is now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+6%3A14-15&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 6:14–15</a> — "You are not under law but under grace." "Shall we sin because we are not under law but under grace? God forbid."</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">The letter kills — the Spirit gives life</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=2+Corinthians+3%3A6&version=WEB" target="_blank">2 Corinthians 3:6</a> — "The letter kills, but the Spirit gives life."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+8%3A2-4&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 8:2–4</a> — "The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus made me free from the law of sin and of death… that the ordinance of the law might be fulfilled in us, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Ezekiel+36%3A26-27&version=WEB" target="_blank">Ezekiel 36:26–27</a> — New heart and Spirit causing obedience.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+5%3A22-23&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 5:22–23</a> — Fruit of the Spirit.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Colossians+2%3A20-23&version=WEB" target="_blank">Colossians 2:20–23</a> — External rule-keeping cannot transform the heart.</div>
  </details>

</details>

<details>
<summary><strong>G. Circumcision, Food Laws, and Sabbath</strong></summary>

  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Circumcision</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+15%3A10-11&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 15:10–11</a> — Peter: "Why do you tempt God by putting a yoke on the neck of the disciples which neither our fathers nor we were able to bear? But we believe that we are saved through the grace of the Lord Jesus, in the same way as they are."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+15%3A19-20&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 15:19–20</a> — The Jerusalem Council's decision: Gentiles are not placed under the Mosaic Law; only minimal practical guidelines given.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+5%3A2-4&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 5:2–4</a> — "If you receive circumcision, Christ will profit you nothing… You are severed from Christ, you who would be justified by the law."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Galatians+6%3A15&version=WEB" target="_blank">Galatians 6:15</a> — "Neither circumcision counts for anything, nor uncircumcision, but a new creation."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Philippians+3%3A3&version=WEB" target="_blank">Philippians 3:3</a> — True circumcision is of the heart by the Spirit.</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Food laws</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Mark+7%3A18-19&version=WEB" target="_blank">Mark 7:18–19</a> — Jesus declared all foods clean.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+10%3A15&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 10:15</a> — "What God has cleansed, you must not call unclean."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+15%3A28-29&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 15:28–29</a> — Law for Gentile believers: the Jerusalem Council required only that Gentiles abstain from food sacrificed to idols, from blood, from strangled animals, and from sexual immorality — the full Mosaic food code was not imposed.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+14%3A14&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 14:14</a> — "Nothing is unclean of itself."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">e. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1+Timothy+4%3A4-5&version=WEB" target="_blank">1 Timothy 4:4–5</a> — "Everything created by God is good… nothing is to be rejected if received with thanksgiving."</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Sabbath and holy days</summary>
    <div class="lv-lc">a. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Colossians+2%3A16-17&version=WEB" target="_blank">Colossians 2:16–17</a> — "Let no one judge you… with respect to a feast day, or a new moon, or a Sabbath day… a shadow of things to come; the body is Christ."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">b. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Romans+14%3A5&version=WEB" target="_blank">Romans 14:5</a> — "One person esteems one day as more sacred than another; another esteems every day alike."</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">c. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Acts+20%3A7&version=WEB" target="_blank">Acts 20:7</a>; <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=1+Corinthians+16%3A2&version=WEB" target="_blank">1 Corinthians 16:2</a> — Believers gathered on the first day of the week.</div>
    <div class="lv-lc">d. <a href="https://www.biblegateway.com/passage/?search=Hebrews+4%3A9-10&version=WEB" target="_blank">Hebrews 4:9–10</a> — A Sabbath rest remains, fulfilled in Christ.</div>
  </details>
  <details>
  <summary class="lv-1">Early Church — Lord's Day Practice</summary>
    <div class="lv-detail">— <strong>Didache</strong> (late 1st/early 2nd c.) — "But every Lord's day gather yourselves together, and break bread, and give thanksgiving… that your sacrifice may be pure."</div>
    <div class="lv-detail">— <strong>Letter of Barnabas</strong> (c. 70–130s AD) — "We keep the eighth day [Sunday] with joyfulness, the day also on which Jesus rose again from the dead."</div>
    <div class="lv-detail">— <strong>Ignatius of Antioch</strong> (c. 110 AD, <em>Letter to the Magnesians</em>) — Christians "no longer observing the Sabbath, but living in the observance of the Lord's Day, on which also our life has sprung up again by Him and by His death."</div>
    <div class="lv-detail">— <strong>Justin Martyr</strong> (c. 150–155 AD, <em>First Apology</em> 67) — "On the day called Sunday, all who live in cities or in the country gather together… Sunday is the day on which we all hold our common assembly, because it is the first day on which God made the world; and Jesus Christ our Savior on the same day rose from the dead."</div>
    <div class="lv-detail">— Sunday gathering for Word, prayer, and Eucharist was the norm across late 1st–mid-2nd century sources. The focus was resurrection theology: Christ rose on the first day, fulfilling and transcending the old covenant.</div>
  </details>

</details>


<details>
<summary><strong>H. Discussion Questions</strong></summary>
<div class="lv-1">1. What was the purpose of the Law, and how does understanding that change how you read the Old Testament?</div>
<div class="lv-1">2. What does it mean practically to be "not under law but under grace"?</div>
<div class="lv-1">3. How does the Holy Spirit fulfill what the Law could not?</div>
<div class="lv-1">4. How should Christians respond to those who insist on keeping Mosaic practices (circumcision, dietary laws, Sabbath)?</div>
</details>

</section>
```
